speaker: interviewer
text: Number four of Leny Bromet. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: The arrival on Amsterdam at the airport was of a dismal, utter despair, and loneliness. There I was standing. I was arriving with some women who are being picked up by relatives. And there was nobody who was picking up me. I stood there in that still, war-destroyed airport feeling-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: --I don't know-- feeling completely black and utterly lost. Because then it hit me that there was nobody there to get me, and nobody-- relative, and why I should actually have returned. I cried so much that there were a taxi-- taxi driver who took pity on me and asked me, where do you want to go? I said, I don't know. So he brought me to a kind of a square there in Amsterdam. And then I remembered the kind woman who supplied supper for us while my mother just died. And I said it was a horrible thing to go back into the street where I lived and where I was being taken away from. But I had no other choice, because I didn't know anybody. So she took me in-- was very nice and was very good to me. After a couple of days she said, well, I'll bring your contact. You should go to that Jewish organization, [NON-ENGLISH]. They looked-- they'll look after you young people. And you should go there, because-- and she was right. There was not much what I can do for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's one incident from a neighbor, which lived beside her and was our next door neighbor, too-- she was a deeply religious family at that time. But-- OK. She met me, and she said, oh, it's you. It's back. She said, you know, I have a suit from your mother. I took it. And-- but you're much too big now for it. I don't think it'll fit you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a nice-- religious woman who should think more considerate about that, what she said. It was no-- no thought of that. It was just an incident, what hit me there. But she hit me with-- you know? Because I know after we were taken out from our house, the neighbors just crawled over the things and stole everything what they could inside. They did it with-- that's another story. So then I was in contact with that organization. And they brought me contact with another-- in an-- into another town where it's a kind of a-- how you call it? An orphan.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] An orphanage now where for boys or girls who had-- who had no family left-- at first in Utrecht-- and I was together with three other girls. The time spent was not so happy. But there, in that way, they more or less tried to prepare me for Israel at that time, which-- or to become a Zionist. Here again, I just followed dutifully rules-- didn't have much time or to think of my own individuality or whatever-- didn't. It was too much walking and-- was being-- being told-- like an automaton, like a machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- the part of my-- my preparation for Israel was to teach me how a household work, naturally. And so I was put to work in a family. He was a professor. His daughter was diabetic.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to weigh the little food. At that time, you had to weigh for the diabetic's portion of food. I had to clean there. And it was so-called my preparation for Israel. The gist behind it is-- with the knowledge I know now, with the papers I had when I came back from Holland-- I got some papers for my past-- that to get all those young people away and go to Israel. They did it there. Well, they did a job. But we went. All three girls-- the four of us, we all-- we all went eventually. So I worked there. Then I went to the big-- a big house in Deventer, another part of Holland. There was a whole group of boys, girls in all-- in preparation for Israel to work. For boys, they-- they lift-- worked in a farm. Other one-- other ones did other jobs. We worked in the house, as you-- [LAUGHS] women do. And-- and there, I met my-- my husband. But then at the time-- I don't know what it was-- I had to be-- they returned me. They sent me back to Utrecht to that home again with the woman. And I forgot the reason why. I still stayed there-- remained there for some time. That was all happening from 1946 till-- let's say January, '48, in that time frame.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then finally, I went back to the house in Deventer. We lived there together. We-- we-- we fought. We argued. We did. We had lessons where we had [NON-ENGLISH] from Israel coming and preparing us, and telling us how it was. In short of the matter, it was-- it was not too bad a time-- a not too bad a time.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I heard that my uncle was alive in Holland, and I visited him. And so I went to-- we-- the time was ready to go to Israel. The organization prepared us. We went on Aliyah Bet. It's called Aliyah Bet. It was semi-illegal-- semi-illegal. Naturally, the English was still in Palestine at that time. I with-- went with a group in January, 1948, to Paris. We remained in Paris for a couple of days in an hotel. Then we went to Marseille. There, we went to another camp, with the whole world together again-- Poles and all the Jews, whoever were ready to go to Israel in what way or another.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a way for the exodus. You have heard of the exodus to go to Israel? And I flatly refused, because it was meaning of Cyprus and being in a camp again. I said, there's no way that I will do that. And where were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Marseille until for four or six weeks, till we-- the time was ready, or the ship was found, or whatever the illegal-- illegal things from the Haganah were ready to do. We sailed-- or sailed, or went to Haifa. And we arrived there. And the British were. We had our passport. Everything was fine and dandy. I mean, they didn't know that it was semi-illegal. Our passports were then given to somebody else, which was not-- for-- [LAUGHS] according to the Dutch government, we weren't allowed to do. We did it. Well, whatever. And our passports were used for other people-- taking our picture out and put the other pictures in. That's why they got lots and lots of people out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Haifa arrival was bad, because it was still under English. And there was still shooting. And the Arabs-- well, the story-- usual story. It doesn't change too much. So we went then, transported to kibbutz. And there in the kibbutz, we were-- lived there til my husband went into the army. I married in the kibbutz. And he went to the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kibbutz didn't have-- it was poor at that time. Now it's one of the richest. [LAUGHS] Not too much food. Nine months, eight months later, I had my first baby. My daughter was born in the kibbutz Na'an-- born in a-- in the hospital in Rehovot. It was a bad time-- shooting and being in the ditch for the-- for Arab attacks. We were close by Ramla, which was an Arab state. And it was a time-- I didn't have now needed to go through that again. Then he came out of the army, and we were a short while in the kibbutz. Kibbutz life didn't appeal to me. It was cooperative. It was together. You had no say again. You see, no, again, no again to-- to-- to-- to assert or whatever, self-- nothing. You're told go there, and work there, and work there. OK. That's wasn't-- [LAUGHS] I didn't take kindly to that. Anyway, he liked to go into-- my husband liked to go into the-- with-- with cows and the-- and the-- and the-- and so on. So we had an opportunity to go to another kibbutz which was just making-- was just being founded. It was close to Haifa. We went there. We came from the rain and the drop-- of the drop and wherever. We came from the worst situation. That kibbutz was even poorer. But a lot of people were there who get parcels from the States.

speaker: interviewee
text: We personally-- I was-- was bad. It didn't pan out. That's kind of a-- a-- a supervision he would've-- should've had over the-- over the-- the cows. Does the cow-- how do you say [NON-ENGLISH]? A supervisor-- he was to be oversee it. It didn't pan out too well. The kibbutz was even poorer than the other one. So I was hungry while I was pregnant for my second daughter. So then the kibbutz left for that place where you originally would follow them. We didn't. We remained underground. And it was poverty galore.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a little shack. There was no water. There was nothing. So he erected some-- made some-- some-- some pipes. And there was a lot of people coming from-- from the illegal immigrants-- for immigrants. They came from-- from Europe, and they settled there in that-- in that place. We lived there for a short while. And he had a job. And he had an accident, and whatever. We lived there. It was not in the-- we're hungry. And it was-- all my weight I gained, I lost there. We had opportunity to go back to Holland. And it was five years later, in 1953. He went in '52 with my-- our eldest daughter back to Israel-- to Holland, where he had a cousin who took us in. And I left a couple of months later in 1953, January '53-- left Israel for Holland. In the meantime, in '50, when our youngest daughter was born, there was a fear of-- polio was rampant there-- scared that she would get it. Luckily, I was, at that time, she didn't get it. It was not a happy situation at that time where we lived in-- no. It was poor, and we lived as gypsies-- no electric light-- nothing. OK. In January '53, we went to Holland, lived for a short while, eight or nine months, with his cousin together. Then somebody from the Jewish agency there supplied us with-- because it was very bad, very hard to get living quarters in Holland, even, you know, it-- it's-- it was bad. They supplied-- she supplied it. It was very helpful. The woman was very helpful over the years til we-- til we-- til we arrived here. And he-- he had a job. And the kids went to school. And I did some cleaning to-- to make a couple of guilders.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the woman I was with in the camp together-- not together, but we knew each other-- she just lived across in the same street. One day, I got to know her. And she said, you know what? I'm going to make apply. I'm going to the States.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she wanted to go first to Canada, because that was not that easy at that time. And then I came home one day. I-- I lost a baby in the meantime. I had a son, and-- and didn't-- come-- died by birth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then all in all, in all, in all the memories and everything else, I said to him, you know what? Why don't we go to Canada? I don't want to be here to be surrounded by memories here and there and everywhere. And then in April '58-- well, it didn't so easy. In April-- in '57, we applied by the Canadian embassy and in Hague. We had an immigration officer who interviewed us and asked if he had-- one of the questions was, did you immigrate before? So he looked at me. I looked there. I said, yes. We-- Where were you before? I said, we were in Israel. And then all of a sudden, he just changed his attitude and had become obnoxious! He became so obnoxious, and he yelled the questions at two-- at us-- that we were totally intimidated like anything else. We didn't need that. That was an embassy official from Canada in the Hague.

speaker: interviewee
text: But at that time, we told him too, I was pregnant. So we had to postpone. He wanted to postpone. A year later, we came back for another immigration officer, and he was a nicest guy who can ever-- because we saw the other one. We were hoping we couldn't get him. He was the nicest guy that I ever set-- ever met. He was naturally asking where's the baby, whatever-- that. He was very, very sensitive and asked. And so we told him it was fine. So that's why we went to Canada. And I'm glad we ever did. And we more or less made a living here. We worked hard. And we made a living. And we are pretty satisfied-- I think-- what we have now-- our lives. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what I want to say. Do you think it's fair that Fritz Philips was accused of cooperating--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: That definitely was not fair. To a certain extent, he had to cooperate Look-- like Schindler had to cooperate too sometimes. Even if he wanted to help us, he had to cooperate in a certain extent, because if he were told-- if he would say, I do it this, and I make materials. Even-- yeah, by the way, the materials what we were working on were inferior and were not too-- were not actually-- they didn't do the German army any good. They were not defective, but they were made in such a way they were not much use.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if he would have told him that, he would himself be killed. No, it's not fair, definite it's not. That's whatever I say. I promote that, because of the Phillips group, I and my other camp inmates are alive today. You told me that-- when we talked before this interview-- that in effect, you felt you had a life sentence. These experiences were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they are. They are. They are, because there is always-- sometimes not-- sometimes daily, sometimes not. My husband and I, we talked the reason. There is no currents like the one. I was two, three years ago visiting our daughter in Kingston. I came back by VIA-- and-- from Toronto to Oakville. And I was sitting, just minding my own business.

speaker: interviewee
text: There comes a snot-nosed, funny conductor-- and tells me I have to leave my seat. I said, why? He said, this is reserved. I said, well, there's no reserved sign on it. I'm, you know-- authority doesn't go well with me, unfortunately, after all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just told him-- I said, well, I-- the reason-- I still don't see any reserved seats, so I don't see why I should leave. He became quite obnoxious and insultive, and he probably didn't like the way I talked to him. He's not used to elderly women maybe talking to him what it is. So he didn't like that. And later on, he threatened to-- if I want to-- I'm here. I can just-- I can just carry you off the train. And you better listen. And he told another one, you watch that woman, because otherwise, she goes off the train. And that was a horrible, horrible, horrible experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband picked me up here from Oakville, and I came crying out of the train. I said, just take it easy. Don't do-- and he was just, is she your-- is she your wife? Well, I don't want that woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was so abusive and insulting. It brought me back-- like authority. I couldn't do anything. I was helpless. I was sitting besides English guys who were on holiday, and just shook their heads. And they said, I've never seen that before. I put my complaint into Via. I got nowhere. I got a lousy letter, and that is all what they did. One of the experience-- what brought you back that whatever you do sometimes-- the Kingston Affair with those women being-- being OK, whatever women they are, being searched and being naked in the cell there-- brought back again the-- the experience that I had been nude and-- and more or less inspected by the Germans. There are little tidbits. In the beginning, when I was going the go train, there was an-- an factory, whatever is import credit. And the-- the smokestack, it had smoked. It's not that any bad anymore, but I couldn't see that smokestack there belching the smoke from there. It's-- it is a lifelong sentence. I mean, I bring it all forward and everything. But I mean, you bury it through the durly-- drudge of life, or whatever you're living, the normal-- you-- you-- it's buried naturally. It shouldn't be on the surface every time, because then I-- I go in the mental institution, in the Clarkson. But as I said, we maintained a semblance of normalcy. I had a job, and we had a job. And we lived a normal life. But there are occasions that you be just thrown-- like the train or-- he'll tell you too from his-- his experience-- that you'll be thrown right back where you were. And then there is no way out. [SIGHS] It is a lifelong-- it's a sentence-- sentence for life! Nobody human being can do that to another one what-- look at abused kids. It's the same thing. Everybody has a scar. And the brain is nice. It's like a wound who is healing. But underneath, it's still going on. And we will have that for the rest of our life-- suspicion and no authority figure. Next time, if he do, he can scream. He can carry me screaming off the bus, because I won't take it. But that's it. That's one of the things, personally for me, the way I-- and the older I get, worse and it even-- worse it gets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you choose to share your story with not only us, but other groups that you've spoken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you. It was not on my own initiative. Sometimes I'm not that initiative. I get schlepped in by my husband. He did it. And I thought it was a good idea. Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you learn in history books is just-- that even our vet said that yesterday-- she-- when we talked. She is very sensitive. She said, when you learn it from a history book, it's nothing. Like you learned from the war, from the south, and the north, in the States, over here, in Canada. It's-- it's your people who have experienced that, well-- OK. That's-- that's-- almost near the end, I think, of-- of telling the story. It's quite-- because even days-- a couple of days after you left. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's repercussions. So this isn't easy for you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, definitely not. It's under me. It's tearing up all the dirt, and all the thoughts, and everything. And you're back again. That's why I decided, or-- to do it too. There is a-- a-- a day again in April, May, I thinks-- in OISE or something, where the students coming from the school. And they're speaking in April, May. We're going there, too. But then it's a very brief story. I can just-- 1/2 an hour, 15 minutes, I can over it. You know, it isn't too-- too bad. But this in depth one-- but was it just once. And that's why I wanted to do the in-depth one.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to thank you very much for sharing your story with us today. And I understand that you have some documents and pictures--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that we will show. And thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for telling us your story today.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my parents. This picture was taken as an ID card for postponing deporting to-- being deported during the war in Holland. It was taken around in the middle of August, 1942-- my father, mother, and myself. Oh, yeah, not bad. This document was sent to me by the-- the-- Dutch International-- Dutch Red Cross, telling me all the dates where I have been in the camp. I have no idea if I requested it or not. The dated-- the letter is dated 1955-- but all the data where I have been in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] This letter was written by two relatives outside the camp of Vught, in the Netherlands. At the beginning, they were allowed to write to-- this is stationery supplied by the camp. And they were allowed to write to relatives or friends outside the camp. Later on, it was revoked. This is the copy in German. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That is Camp Vught stationery. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Sweden. We were-- while we are-- we're recovering. And there was-- as a treat-- to have a sightseeing tour of the harbor of Goteborg, in Sweden. Part of our group-- there is another boat that has another part of the women. This is what I have. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was by my arrival in Sweden. And it was around, I would say, April-- around April, 1945, with my hair barely grown-- but growing nevertheless-- our arrival in Sweden in the town of Malmo. Oh, that was the-- speaks for itself-- the Allied Expedition Card-- DP card-- DP index card-- what was issued to me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think the scan means maybe Scandinavia, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my-- this my-- card-- identity card on arriving in the government of Palestine in February, 1948, issued by the British and the-- and the Israeli [NON-ENGLISH]. That's the picture. This is pictures of the gravestone of my mother in a cemetery of Holland. My daughter is standing in front of that, while visiting a couple of years ago. And besides there is the gravestone of my grandmother, who died in 1941. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's a picture of the gravestone. That's all what's left on pictures for my grandparents. In May 1990, we had a reunion of our Philips group. And I went to Holland. And this is one of the pictures. We were united with most of the women. Not all of them attended the meeting. And it was naturally a lot of memories. And the third of the right-- or the third of the left, is engineer Fritz Philips, who was originating in our Philips group and the factory in Vught. The first on the left is his wife. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the other side of the letter we showed earlier, sent for-- prisoners or inmates were allowed to send to relatives. The other side-- what is the printed is is in English, is the your return address and prisoner so and so, number so and so. That's the number they had to wear on their coat, and what block they were, what barrack they were. And the other was K.L. [NON-ENGLISH] is the name. [NON-ENGLISH]. And K.L. Means concentration camp. Translated in German means concentration lager [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH] means prisoner.
